title: manager-prompt-placeholder-leak
description:
This is a continuation of manager-prompt-placeholder-leak. A factory-internal prompt placeholder leak recurred again in the current window, with the flagged watcher note reporting high-severity placeholder markers in a manager_watcher prompt at 2026-05-29T18:46:20.445997+00:00. The issue remains attributable to prompt assembly rather than story-domain behavior, and it is appearing across consecutive watcher intervals without evidence of resolution.

Operational impact in this window was otherwise low: there were no failed runs, retry clusters, queue snapshots, spend spikes, or tick anomalies recorded. The affected manager_watcher run completed successfully at 2026-05-29T18:46:28.243894+00:00, so the current evidence shows a persistent plumbing defect rather than an immediate execution failure. That said, leaked placeholders in manager prompts are a clear factory-side correctness issue and continued recurrence means the prior escalation has not yet cleared.

Possible explanations remain limited to factory prompt construction or dispatch-layer handling, because the detector explicitly classifies placeholder markers as a prompt-log leak and the recurrence is on an internal manager persona rather than a story-specific workflow. The unchanged missing-DB worktrees and review churn observations are separate background conditions and do not materially change this concern.

evidence.summary_excerpt: The previously escalated prompt placeholder leak recurred at the start of this window in a manager_watcher prompt, while the long-standing set of missing-DB worktrees remains unchanged.

evidence.excerpt: {"noteworthy":"High-severity placeholder markers were found again in a manager_watcher prompt at 2026-05-29T18:46:20.445997+00:00, continuing the previously escalated prompt assembly issue."}

escalation_reason: Recurring manager prompt placeholder leak persists across consecutive watcher intervals through 2026-05-29T18:46:20.445997+00:00. This is a clear factory-side prompt assembly/dispatch pattern rather than a story-domain issue, and L3 judgment is needed because the prior escalation has not resolved the defect.
